sections.title: Core Idea
points: Module 10 focuses on monitoring AWS resources, auditing activity, meeting compliance requirements, and governing cloud environments.
The main flow is Secure → Monitor → Audit → Compliance.
AWS provides services to track performance, record actions, evaluate configurations, manage accounts, and optimize resources.
sections.exam_tip: AWS exams often test which service fits monitoring, auditing, compliance, or governance scenarios.

sections.title: Governance and Compliance Flow
points: Secure = protect data, systems, and infrastructure.
Monitor = continuously observe activity, performance, traffic, and events.
Audit = review activity and check whether requirements are being followed.
Compliance = meet regulations, standards, and internal policies.
sections.exam_tip: Know the difference between monitoring, auditing, and compliance.

sections.title: Amazon CloudWatch
points: Monitors AWS resources and applications in real time.
Collects metrics from AWS resources, applications, and on-premises servers.
Can create alarms when metrics cross thresholds.
Provides dashboards for a single view of metrics and alarms.
Collects and analyzes logs from systems, applications, and AWS services.
sections.exam_tip: Need metrics, alarms, dashboards, logs, or EC2 utilization monitoring = CloudWatch.

sections.title: CloudWatch Metrics
points: Metrics are data points tied to resources.
Examples include EC2 CPU utilization, network traffic, and application performance data.
CloudWatch uses metrics to track resource health and performance.
sections.exam_tip: Performance data point = CloudWatch Metric.

sections.title: CloudWatch Alarms
points: Alarms watch metrics and compare them to thresholds.
Can send notifications when thresholds are reached.
Can trigger automated actions, such as scaling resources.
sections.exam_tip: Threshold + notification or action = CloudWatch Alarm.

sections.title: CloudWatch Dashboards
points: Dashboards are customizable visual pages in the CloudWatch console.
They show metrics, alarms, and operational data in one place.
Useful for monitoring multiple resources from a single view.
sections.exam_tip: Need one visual monitoring page = CloudWatch Dashboard.

sections.title: CloudWatch Logs
points: Centralizes logs from applications, systems, and AWS services.
Can collect, store, search, filter, and analyze log files.
Useful for troubleshooting application errors and performance issues.
sections.exam_tip: Need application logs or log analysis = CloudWatch Logs.

sections.title: AWS CloudTrail
points: Records user activity and API calls across AWS services.
Helps answer who did what, where, and when.
Used for auditing, security monitoring, operational troubleshooting, and compliance.
Can deliver log files to Amazon S3 for retention and audit evidence.
sections.exam_tip: Need to know who changed something or track API activity = CloudTrail.

sections.title: CloudTrail Events
points: Events capture actions performed in an AWS account.
Examples include API calls, console actions, and management events.
Event history provides a searchable record of recent management events.
sections.exam_tip: Recent searchable record of AWS actions = CloudTrail Event History.

sections.title: CloudTrail Logs
points: CloudTrail can deliver event logs to an Amazon S3 bucket.
These logs can be retained for auditing and compliance.
CloudTrail logs help provide evidence of AWS account activity.
sections.exam_tip: Need API activity files stored in S3 = CloudTrail Logs.

sections.title: CloudTrail Insights
points: Analyzes normal API call patterns.
Detects unusual API call volume or API error rates.
Generates events when activity differs from normal behavior.
sections.exam_tip: Unusual API activity or anomaly detection = CloudTrail Insights.

sections.title: AWS Artifact
points: Provides no-cost, on-demand access to AWS security and compliance reports.
Provides access to select online agreements.
Helps customers assess AWS compliance and provide documentation to auditors.
sections.exam_tip: Need AWS compliance reports or agreements = AWS Artifact.

sections.title: AWS Artifact Reports
points: Provides compliance reports from third-party auditors.
Shows that AWS meets different global, regional, and industry-specific standards.
Can be shared with auditors or regulators as evidence of AWS controls.
sections.exam_tip: Need third-party AWS compliance reports = Artifact Reports.

sections.title: AWS Artifact Agreements
points: Lets customers review, accept, and manage agreements with AWS.
Can manage agreements for individual accounts or accounts in AWS Organizations.
Useful for regulated workloads such as HIPAA-related requirements.
sections.exam_tip: Need to accept or manage AWS agreements = Artifact Agreements.

sections.title: AWS Config
points: Assesses, audits, and evaluates AWS resource configurations.
Tracks resource configuration changes over time.
Checks whether resources follow required company rules or compliance standards.
Can identify noncompliant resources and support remediation.
sections.exam_tip: Need to check if resources are configured correctly = AWS Config.

sections.title: AWS Audit Manager
points: Continually audits AWS usage to simplify risk and compliance assessments.
Automates evidence collection for audits.
Helps create audit-ready reports with less manual effort.
Useful when teams need proof that controls are operating effectively.
sections.exam_tip: Need automated audit evidence collection = AWS Audit Manager.

sections.title: AWS Organizations
points: Centrally manages multiple AWS accounts.
Supports consolidated billing across accounts.
Uses organizational units, or OUs, to group accounts.
Uses service control policies, or SCPs, to set maximum permissions.
Can help manage access, security, compliance, and billing at scale.
sections.exam_tip: Multiple AWS accounts, consolidated billing, OUs, or SCPs = AWS Organizations.

sections.title: AWS Organizations Key Terms
points: Management account = central account that creates and manages the organization.
Member account = account that belongs to the organization.
Organizational unit = logical group of accounts.
Service control policy = policy that sets maximum permissions for accounts or OUs.
Root = top-level parent container for the organization.
sections.exam_tip: SCPs apply to OUs and member accounts, not directly to IAM users, groups, or roles.

sections.title: AWS Control Tower
points: Helps set up and govern a secure, compliant, multi-account AWS environment.
Uses best practices for security, operations, and compliance.
Provides guardrails, dashboards, account provisioning, and governance automation.
Works well for organizations that need standardized account setup.
sections.exam_tip: Need governed multi-account setup with best practices = AWS Control Tower.

sections.title: AWS Control Tower Features
points: Landing zone = enterprise-wide multi-account environment based on best practices.
Account Factory = standardized account creation template.
Controls or guardrails = high-level governance rules.
Dashboard = visual view of accounts, controls, and compliance status.
sections.exam_tip: Landing zone, guardrails, and Account Factory are Control Tower clues.

sections.title: AWS Service Catalog
points: Lets organizations create, share, and manage approved catalogs of AWS resources.
Allows users to deploy approved resources through self-service.
Helps maintain governance while speeding up resource provisioning.
Useful when employees need approved templates instead of creating resources manually.
sections.exam_tip: Approved resource menu or curated catalog = AWS Service Catalog.

sections.title: AWS License Manager
points: Helps manage software licenses and licensing costs.
Supports Bring Your Own License, or BYOL, scenarios.
Tracks license usage and enforces license limits.
Helps reduce the risk of software license noncompliance.
sections.exam_tip: BYOL, software licenses, or license limits = AWS License Manager.

sections.title: AWS Trusted Advisor
points: Continuously evaluates AWS environments using best-practice checks.
Provides recommendations for cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits.
Can identify underutilized resources, security risks, missing backups, and approaching limits.
Helps customers optimize AWS resources at scale.
sections.exam_tip: Need best-practice recommendations across cost, security, performance, resilience, and limits = Trusted Advisor.

sections.title: AWS Health
points: Provides information about events and changes affecting AWS resources.
AWS Health Dashboard shows account-specific health information.
Can notify customers about service events, planned changes, and account notifications.
Useful for troubleshooting incidents and planning for lifecycle events.
sections.exam_tip: Account-specific AWS service events or planned changes = AWS Health.

sections.title: IAM Access Analyzer
points: Helps identify external access to AWS resources.
Validates IAM policies against security standards.
Helps refine permissions toward least privilege.
Can detect broad or unused access.
sections.exam_tip: Least privilege, external access, or IAM policy validation = IAM Access Analyzer.

sections.title: Customer Compliance Center
points: Provides AWS compliance learning resources.
Includes customer compliance stories and answers to common compliance questions.
Includes auditing security checklists and compliance guidance.
sections.exam_tip: Need compliance resources and guidance, not reports = Customer Compliance Center.

sections.title: Common Exam Scenarios
points: Monitor EC2 CPU utilization = CloudWatch.
Send alert when utilization crosses a threshold = CloudWatch Alarm.
Collect application logs = CloudWatch Logs.
Find out who changed a resource = CloudTrail.
Store API activity logs in S3 = CloudTrail Logs.
Download AWS compliance reports = AWS Artifact.
Check if resources follow required configurations = AWS Config.
Automate audit evidence collection = AWS Audit Manager.
Manage multiple accounts and billing = AWS Organizations.
Set up governed multi-account environment = AWS Control Tower.
sections.exam_tip: Look for the action word: monitor, audit, comply, govern, optimize.

sections.title: Common Exam Traps
points: CloudWatch monitors performance, but CloudTrail records API activity.
AWS Config checks resource configurations, but CloudTrail records who made changes.
AWS Artifact gives reports and agreements, but Audit Manager collects audit evidence.
AWS Organizations manages accounts, but Control Tower governs and sets up a best-practice multi-account environment.
Service Catalog provides approved resources, but License Manager manages software licenses.
Trusted Advisor recommends improvements, but AWS Health reports AWS events affecting your resources.
sections.exam_tip: Many services sound similar, so focus on the exact job each one performs.

sections.title: Quick Memory Map
points: CloudWatch = monitor metrics and logs
CloudTrail = record API actions
Artifact = compliance reports and agreements
Config = resource configuration compliance
Audit Manager = audit evidence collection
Organizations = multiple accounts and billing
Control Tower = governed multi-account setup
Service Catalog = approved resource catalog
License Manager = software licenses and BYOL
Trusted Advisor = best-practice recommendations
AWS Health = account-specific service events
IAM Access Analyzer = least privilege access analysis
sections.exam_tip: Memorize this map cold before taking the quiz.
